reason: Aura3D's screenshot reads clearly as a neon tunnel flythrough: receding emissive ring segments in cyan/pink/amber/purple, a dark depth-falloff vanishing point, and a central glow core, satisfying all required visual evidence (tunnel interior, emissive segments, bloom, fog/depth, flythrough framing). Its only flaw is a few stray dark diagonal streaks from the longitudinal wall-guide boxes. The Three.js screenshot is dominated by grossly overblown bloom (UnrealBloom 1.35 + ACES exposure 1.18) that washes the frame to a near-white lavender haze; only a faint central spiral hints at tunnel structure and emissive segments/depth are not clearly readable, so it underdelivers on the prompt despite showing bloom. Source-wise Aura is markedly more modular: ~164 lines of declarative high-level @aura3d/engine composition (scene builder, camera.dolly, effects.bloom/fog, timeline.loop) vs Three.js's 265 lines of low-level manual shader/panel/composer wiring. Both compile, run in browser, exit 0, with no timeout or repair turns. Note the screenshots and metrics broadly agree; the only tension is Three.js's metrics report a successful render while its image is visually degraded by over-bloom rather than failing outright.
